L0_description: The jumper chose to exit in strong, sustained wind conditions that were clearly observable and assessed as dangerous by companions, two of whom declined to jump. The wind conditions were not unforeseeable; they were present, worsening, and explicitly warned against. The decision to jump in conditions that overpowered the canopy's ability to penetrate constitutes a human error in judgment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in excessive wind
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to exit in strong, sustained wind conditions that multiple companions judged unacceptable, after waiting 45 minutes during which conditions worsened. The wind overpowered his canopy's forward penetration capability, pushing him back into the cliff wall. The decision to jump in these conditions was the direct producing mechanism of the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was on his last day in Lauterbrunnen and described as 'desperate' to get one final jump before his flight home the next morning. This time pressure and desire to not waste the trip drove him to proceed despite conditions that his companions judged unacceptable and despite being explicitly told it was not worth it.

L0_description: The bridle was misrouted around the right leg strap during packing/rigging, preventing canopy extraction and causing a total malfunction (PC in tow). The narrative explicitly states the jumper rushed packing the night before and that neither the jumper nor his companion performed a gear check that would have immediately caught the routing error. This constitutes human error in both rigging and pre-jump inspection.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle misrouted during packing
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper routed the bridle over the right leg strap during packing, creating a configuration where the pilot chute could not extract the canopy from the container. This rigging error directly produced a total malfunction (PC in tow) that resulted in closed-container impact. The misrouting was a technique/attention failure during an otherwise routine packing procedure, not a deliberate decision to route the bridle incorrectly.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: Neither the jumper nor his partner performed a pre-jump gear check at the exit point. The narrative explicitly states that running a hand along the bridle routing would have immediately caught the misrouted bridle. This omission of a standard pre-jump verification allowed the packing error to go undetected.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention during packing
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper packed in a rush because he wanted to go to sleep early, indicating reduced attentiveness during the packing process. This degraded attention management during a critical rigging task contributed to the bridle being misrouted over the leg strap without detection.

L0_description: The jumper chose to fly a wingsuit proximity line in flat-light conditions (snow, no shadows, no contrast, no texture) that eliminated depth perception cues, directly causing fatal loss of altitude awareness. These conditions were observable before the jump — heavy fresh snow, low-level clouds, and complete absence of contrast are assessable environmental factors that a competent BASE jumper could identify as dangerous for proximity flying. The decision to jump in these conditions constitutes a human error rather than an unforeseeable environmental event.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Visual illusion from flat light
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew into terrain at full speed with no deployment initiation or corrective body position change. The narrative explicitly identifies complete flat light conditions (heavy fresh snow, no shadows, no contrast, no texture on the snowfield) as eliminating all depth cues, causing him to misperceive his altitude as much higher than it actually was. He acted consistently with his false perception—maintaining a relaxed posture with no urgency—and never initiated deployment because he believed he had plenty of altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The complete flat light conditions (heavy fresh snow, no shadows, no contrast, no texture) caused the jumper's visual sensory system to fail to correctly perceive his altitude and proximity to terrain. This spatial disorientation was the physiological mechanism underlying the Layer 1 perceptual error, as his depth perception was entirely defeated by the absence of visual cues.

L2_preconditions.label: Flat light eliminated depth cues
L2_preconditions.description: Heavy fresh snow with no shadows, no contrast, and no texture on the snowfield created complete flat light conditions that actively degraded the jumper's ability to perceive altitude during flight. This environmental condition directly impaired his visual performance, making it impossible to judge proximity to the snow-covered terrain and enabling the perceptual error.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper maintained a relaxed flight posture focused on flying his line with no apparent cross-checking of altitude through alternative cues (terrain features, elapsed time, altimeter if equipped). His attention appears to have been channelized on the flight path and body position rather than actively monitoring altitude through whatever secondary cues might have been available in the flat-light environment.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate flight planning for flat light
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper proceeded with a wingsuit proximity flight on a day with heavy fresh snow, low-level clouds, and complete flat light—conditions that eliminated the visual depth cues essential for terrain proximity flying. Accepted practice requires conditions to be near-perfect for proximity flight, and flying near terrain without reliable depth perception represents a failure in jump preparation and go/no-go assessment for the specific conditions encountered.

L0_description: The narrative provides positive evidence that the fatality resulted from a manufacturing defect in the harness webbing (incorrect heat treatment causing internal degradation undetectable through normal inspection). The jumper's packing, rigging, maintenance, equipment selection, and execution were all explicitly described as correct. The equipment failure was independent of any human error, constituting an unforeseeable material failure.

The narrative provides positive evidence of an unforeseeable environmental event: a massive localized rotor caused by a temperature inversion layer and differential heating that collapsed the canopy post-opening. Meteorologists confirmed the event could not have been predicted or detected from the exit point. The jumper's exit, tracking, deployment, and packing were all described as correct, and no wind indicators showed any movement before or after the jump. This meets the criteria for an environmental factor not reasonably identifiable by the jumper.

The fatality resulted from a massive brainstem stroke caused by a congenital arterial malformation that had never produced symptoms and was undetected on a recent physical. The jumper had no prior medical history or diagnosis that would have made this event foreseeable. Equipment was recovered in perfect condition, and the jump was conducted in perfect conditions by a highly experienced jumper, confirming a sudden medical event outside the jumper's reasonable anticipation or control.

The narrative explicitly identifies two human errors: the jumper chose to jump in wind conditions (20 knots sustained, gusts over 30) that four experienced companions deemed unsafe and stood down from, and he failed to prioritize deploying his pilot chute over recovering stability after an unstable exit. The wind conditions were observable and forecastable, and the group's decision to stand down confirms they were identifiable as unsafe before the jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in unsafe wind conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump in 20-knot sustained winds with gusts over 30 knots from the left, conditions that four experienced companions had unanimously deemed unsafe and stood down from. This decision to launch into a strong crosswind directly produced the fatal outcome by placing the jumper in conditions where wind pushed him sideways and back toward the wall, making survival dependent on flawless execution with essentially no margin for error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After an unstable exit caused by the crosswind gust, the jumper tumbled along the face for three to four seconds and never got a hand to his pilot chute. Regardless of the poor exit and turbulent conditions, the priority order dictates deploying the parachute above all else. The jumper failed to execute any deployment action during the available time before striking a ledge approximately 80 meters below the exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Four experienced companions unanimously assessed the conditions as unsafe and stood down. Renzo's decision to remain and ultimately jump suggests he unreasonably overestimated his own capability to handle conditions that peers with equal or greater experience rejected. This overconfidence in his ability to manage 20-knot sustained crosswinds with 30+ knot gusts contributed directly to the decision to jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong crosswind degraded exit stability
L2_preconditions.description: The strong crosswind gust caught the jumper immediately on exit, pushing him sideways and back toward the wall and causing him to go from stable to head-low within the first second. This violent environmental force directly degraded his ability to achieve or maintain a body position from which he could reach his pilot chute, contributing to his failure to deploy during the available time before impact.

L0_description: Multiple human errors contributed to this fatality. The jumper chose to jump while severely impaired from heavy alcohol consumption the night before (vomiting, unsteady, splitting headache), which degraded his physical coordination and led to an unstable exit. He then failed to deploy his pilot chute despite approximately 6 seconds of freefall, fixating on stability recovery rather than prioritizing deployment—a fundamental violation of the accepted practice that deploying unstable is preferable to impact with nothing out.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper fixated on recovering stability during freefall and never extracted the pilot chute from the BOC. He impacted the talus approximately 6 seconds after exit with the PC still stowed. Even from an unstable position, deploying the parachute was the priority action that could have prevented the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had been drinking heavily until 4am the night before, was visibly still impaired on the hike (splitting headache, vomiting twice, visibly unsteady), and the narrative explicitly states the hangover affected his coordination. This degraded physical state contributed to the buckled-leg exit that produced the instability from which he then failed to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper fixated entirely on recovering stability instead of deploying the pilot chute. He channelized his attention on regaining a belly-to-earth position throughout the available freefall, excluding the higher-priority task of deploying the parachute from any attitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that jumping hung over was not unusual for the jumper and that he had done so before without issues. This represents a sustained pattern of jumping while impaired by alcohol aftereffects, normalizing a known deficiency rather than correcting it. This pattern enabled the conditions on this specific jump that contributed to the unstable exit and subsequent failure to deploy.

L0_description: Multiple human errors contributed to this fatality. The jumper used a tracking suit on a cliff with only two skydives in it and zero cliff jumps with it, far below the accepted minimum of 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use. He chose an intermediate-to-advanced exit requiring effective tracking despite having only 40 BASE jumps from lower exits and no slider-up cliff experience with the suit. Additionally, he drove 14 hours through the night and was visibly exhausted, degrading physical and cognitive performance. He disregarded a more experienced jumper's suggestion to start with a more forgiving exit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to achieve effective tracking flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited and attempted to fly a tracking suit to gain separation from the cliff, but was unable to achieve forward drive, instead falling at a steep angle. He impacted a ledge approximately 7 seconds after exit without achieving adequate separation. The plan (exit, track away, deploy) was appropriate, but his physical execution of tracking suit flight failed — the suit was 'flying him, not the other way around.'

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking suit experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only two skydives in the tracking suit and had never jumped it from a cliff. Accepted practice requires mastering a tracking suit in skydiving (minimum 40-50 tracking suit skydives) before any BASE use. His near-total lack of tracking suit experience meant he did not have the body position skills or muscle memory to achieve effective forward drive, directly causing the skill-based execution failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue from overnight drive
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove 14 hours straight through the night and was visibly exhausted at the exit point. Sleep deprivation and physical fatigue degrade coordination, reaction time, and the ability to execute precise body position control, contributing to his inability to fly the tracking suit effectively.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence from video observation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper dismissed a suggestion to start with a more forgiving exit, stating the chosen exit 'looked easy from the footage.' This indicates he overestimated his ability to fly the exit based on watching videos rather than actual experience with the tracking suit in a cliff environment, contributing to his proceeding with a task beyond his demonstrated capability.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading on a static line jump from a building and attempted correction by grabbing only the right toggle and pulling it hard, which drove her in a diving turn into the building. Per accepted practice, the correct response to a 180° off-heading facing the object is to use rear risers (both rear risers to halt forward speed, then release one for a controlled turn), not a single hard toggle input which creates a diving turn toward the object. This incorrect emergency response technique constitutes a human error that directly contributed to the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wrong correction procedure applied
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a 180° off-heading opening facing the building, the jumper pulled a single brake toggle hard rather than using rear risers to halt forward speed and then turn. Pulling one toggle in a diving turn drove her directly into the building. The accepted procedure for a 180° off-heading is to input both rear risers to stop forward movement, then release one to turn, or to grab both toggles and pull to near-stall before releasing one side. Applying aggressive single-toggle input created a diving turn into the object, producing the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient off-heading correction training
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps over 3 seasons, the jumper's reaction to a 180° off-heading—going straight for toggles and pulling only one hard—indicates she had not developed the trained muscle memory for the accepted rear-riser correction procedure. Accepted practice requires extensive practice of instant heading corrections (rear riser and brake techniques) at simple spans before progressing to more demanding objects. Her response suggests insufficient drilling of the correct 180° correction sequence.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized attention on single toggle
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states she 'found only the right one' toggle, indicating her attention channelized on locating and using the first toggle she could grab rather than shifting to the rear risers—a larger, easier-to-grab target. This fixation on the toggle search excluded consideration of the rear-riser alternative, which is the accepted primary correction method for a 180° off-heading near an object.

L0_description: The jumper chose to exit in near-zero visibility conditions (fog obscuring the wall, fjord, and landing area) when all other jumpers declined and packed up to hike down. Jumping in conditions where terrain hazards are invisible is a clear departure from accepted practice, which requires evaluating terrain, weather, and landing area visibility before jumping. The jumper's decision to rely on memory and 'feel' rather than visual reference led to impact with an unseen ledge, constituting a human error in judgment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in zero visibility conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to exit in essentially zero visibility (fog obscuring the wall, fjord, and landing area) when all other jumpers chose to hike down. He believed he could navigate by feel and memory alone, but this decision to jump in conditions that precluded seeing terrain was the direct producing mechanism of the fatal outcome. He tracked into an unseen protruding ledge approximately 120 meters below the exit, impacting in terminal freefall with his pilot chute still stowed.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper explicitly stated he had done this exit dozens of times and 'knew the heading by feel,' expressing unreasonable confidence that memory and proprioception could substitute for visual terrain awareness in zero-visibility conditions. This overestimation of his own capability to navigate blind directly drove his decision to jump when all other jumpers recognized the conditions as unjumpable and chose to hike down.

L0_description: The jumper reached up to adjust his helmet-mounted GoPro during freefall tracking, which caused him to lose his tracking body position and go flat. This distraction from filming resulted in loss of separation from the wall, drift toward the object, and failure to deploy his parachute, leading to terminal impact. Adjusting a camera mid-flight is a clear human error involving distraction and loss of situational awareness.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Adjusted camera mid-flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to reach up and adjust his helmet-mounted GoPro during an active tracking flight near terrain. This deliberate action broke his tracking position, caused him to go flat, and eliminated his separation from the wall. The decision to prioritize camera adjustment over maintaining the track was the initiating mechanism that produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper never deployed his parachute despite drifting toward a visible ledge for several seconds. Regardless of the camera adjustment that degraded his track, he had time to recognize the approaching terrain and initiate deployment or corrective action, but failed to do so. This omission was the direct mechanism of death by terminal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's attention was channelized on the camera adjustment task, causing him to lose situational awareness of his proximity to terrain. Despite the ledge being in clear view for several seconds, he made no corrective input or deployment attempt, indicating his attention remained fixed on the camera problem rather than monitoring his flight path and altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was serving as the cameraman on a two-way jump, creating a motivation to capture footage that drove him to prioritize adjusting his GoPro mid-flight over maintaining his tracking position and terrain separation. The filming role provided an identifiable motivation separate from safe flight execution that led to the decision to adjust the camera during an active track near terrain.

L0_description: The jumper had a recognized, recurring problem with angle of attack control leading to repeated near-miss stalls. He acknowledged the need for remedial skydiving training but continued jumping cliffs without addressing the deficiency. The fatal stall and terrain impact resulted directly from this known skill gap and the decision to continue proximity flying without corrective action.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of AoA control causing stall
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Approximately 15 seconds into the flight, the jumper allowed his angle of attack to become too high, causing the wingsuit to stall and his glide path to transition from shallow to nearly vertical. This was a known, recurring execution failure — he had experienced at least four prior near-miss stalls from the same technique error. The jumper understood the correct technique but failed to maintain proper AoA, which directly produced the fatal impact with terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known AoA deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper experienced at least four near-miss stalls from the same AoA control error over the preceding three months. He explicitly acknowledged the problem, told multiple people he needed to return to skydiving for dedicated stall recovery practice, and never did so. He continued jumping cliffs every weekend without addressing the known deficiency, normalizing a recurring life-threatening execution failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient AoA control skill for proximity flight
L2_preconditions.description: Despite 80 wingsuit BASE jumps and 300 wingsuit skydives, the jumper's AoA control was demonstrably insufficient for the proximity terrain flying he was undertaking. The recurring near-miss stalls — at least four in three months — indicate his skill level had not developed to match the demands of the flights he was attempting. His own assessment that he needed additional skydiving practice confirms the training gap.

L0_description: The jumper failed to execute any heading correction (riser or toggle input) after a 90-degree off-heading opening, freezing with arms at sides despite approximately two seconds of available reaction time before striking guy wires. This failure to act constitutes a human error. Additionally, the jumper had minimal antenna experience (only two prior antenna jumps from taller/more forgiving structures) and was jumping at night, representing a progression and decision-making concern for a 60-jump BASE jumper on a technical object.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to perform off-heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper's canopy opened with a 90-degree off-heading pointing directly at guy wires approximately 40 feet away, yet he made no toggle input, no riser input, and kept his arms at his sides. Multiple witnesses confirmed he took no corrective action during the approximately two seconds available before striking the guy wires. The appropriate action (immediate riser or brake input to correct heading or halt forward flight) was known and available but was not executed, consistent with a freeze response — a failure in cognitive/motor execution rather than a wrong decision.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple witnesses stated he 'completely froze the moment he saw the wires directly in front of him,' with arms remaining at his sides and no corrective input attempted during the approximately two seconds available. This automatic physiological freeze response upon seeing the guy wires directly ahead rendered him unable to execute the known corrective actions (riser or brake input) that constituted the Layer 1 skill-based error.

L2_preconditions.label: Limited antenna experience for site demands
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 60 BASE jumps and just two prior antenna jumps, both from taller structures with guy wires further from the exit point. This limited antenna-specific experience meant he had not developed the practiced, reflexive off-heading correction responses needed for the tight heading tolerances of a 900-foot antenna with guy wires approximately 40 feet from the flight path. Insufficient muscle memory for immediate corrective action in this environment contributed to his inability to respond when the off-heading occurred.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Witnesses reported he 'just watched the wires come at him' with no corrective action. This fixation on the approaching obstacle — consistent with object fixation as described in accepted practice — channelized his attention on the threat rather than on executing the corrective response, contributing to the complete absence of riser or toggle input.

L0_description: The jumper knowingly used a pilot chute with a large tear in the mesh after being advised by companions to replace it and offered spare equipment. This decision to jump with visibly damaged equipment constitutes a human error in equipment maintenance and pre-jump inspection, directly causing a PC failure (inability to inflate), resulting in a total malfunction and closed-container impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped with known-damaged pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was aware that his pilot chute had a large tear in the mesh from the previous jump. Despite being told it needed replacement and being offered spare pilot chutes by other jumpers, he decided the damaged PC would still function adequately. This decision directly produced the fatal outcome: the torn PC could not hold air, collapsed in his burble, and resulted in a total malfunction (closed-container impact).

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper overestimated his ability to assess the damaged PC's functionality, dismissing explicit warnings from peers and offers of replacement equipment. His confidence that the torn PC would 'still catch enough air' reflects an unreasonable overestimation of the equipment's capability despite clear evidence of significant damage.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly describes multiple human errors contributing to the fatality: the group failed to establish a proper separation plan, exit order delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes for the multi-way jump. David tracked directly over the second jumper and deployed into that jumper's inflating canopy without checking his airspace, resulting in a fatal canopy entanglement. These failures in planning and situational awareness during a multi-way jump constitute clear human error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to check airspace before deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David deployed his pilot chute directly into the second jumper's inflating canopy without checking his airspace. The narrative explicitly states he did not check his airspace before deploying. This was an attention failure — the correct action in a multi-way jump is to maintain awareness of other jumpers and ensure clear airspace before deployment, but David failed to execute this check, resulting in canopy entanglement and fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate multi-way separation planning
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that nobody properly went through exit order or separation plan, no specific delays were set, and no one discussed flight paths or opening altitudes. This failure to establish a proper multi-way plan created the conditions where two jumpers could end up at the same altitude and position, directly enabling the airspace-check failure that led to the entanglement.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention to airspace awareness
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states David did not check his airspace before deploying. This represents a failure in attention management — he failed to maintain situational awareness of other jumpers' positions during a multi-way jump, which is a critical attention task required before deployment. This inattention directly enabled the skill-based error of deploying into the second jumper's canopy.

L0_description: The jumper deliberately delayed pilot chute extraction too long, pulling at an altitude insufficient for canopy inflation. The narrative explicitly states he had a pattern of progressively extending delays beyond safe limits despite warnings from other jumpers, and that on this jump he 'simply pushed it one second too far.' This constitutes a clear human error in deployment timing.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Intentional low pull beyond safe altitude
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deliberately chose to delay deployment beyond safe altitude, pulling very low just above the water. This was a conscious decision consistent with a pattern of progressively extending delays from 4 to 8+ seconds. The canopy was still extracting from the container at impact, meaning the chosen pull altitude was insufficient for the parachute to open regardless of execution quality.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's progressive extension of delays from 4 to 6 to 8+ seconds over the past season, despite repeated warnings from other jumpers, indicates he unreasonably overestimated his ability to judge minimum safe pull altitude. This overconfidence in his timing and margin estimation directly contributed to the decision to delay deployment beyond safe altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple other jumpers had spoken to him about his progressively longer delays on multiple occasions. Despite this repeated external feedback identifying a dangerous pattern, he failed to take corrective action and continued extending his delays. This sustained pattern of ignoring known risk directly enabled the fatal low pull decision.

L2_preconditions.label: Personality Style
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes an enduring behavioral pattern of progressively pushing delays longer and longer over an entire season, from 4 to 6 to 8+ seconds, despite repeated warnings from peers. This stable pattern of limit-pushing behavior contributed to the decision to pull one second too far on the fatal jump.

L0_description: The jumper flew an expert-level wingsuit (Aura 1) with only approximately 10 skydives in it before using it for BASE, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 suit-specific skydives. This premature upgrade to a high-performance suit he had not mastered led to an in-flight stall from which he could not recover, resulting in terminal impact. This constitutes human error in equipment selection and progression.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to maintain adequate airspeed and AoA
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper's angle of attack shifted mid-flight, causing the wingsuit to stall. His glide path went from shallow to nearly vertical over approximately two seconds, and he sank out rapidly without recovering. The intended course of action (flying the intermediate line) was appropriate, but he failed to maintain the correct AoA and airspeed to sustain flight, resulting in an unrecoverable stall and terrain impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient experience with expert wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only approximately 10 skydives in the Aura 1 expert-level wingsuit before taking it off a cliff. Accepted practice requires 40-60 wingsuit skydives with the specific suit before BASE use. An expert wingsuit has stall boundaries requiring many flights to understand, and the jumper had not put in that time, leaving him without the skill and familiarity needed to maintain adequate airspeed and AoA during the flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Premature upgrade to expert wingsuit for BASE
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper configured his jump with an expert-level wingsuit (Aura 1) despite having only approximately 10 skydives in it and only 40 wingsuit BASE jumps total with intermediate suits. Accepted practice states that even an entry-level suit requires 30-80 BASE jumps to reach full potential before considering an upgrade, and the specific suit should have 40-60 skydives before BASE use. This equipment selection created the conditions under which the skill-based error occurred, as the expert suit's narrower flight envelope and different stall characteristics exceeded his demonstrated proficiency.

L0_description: The jumper had only 5 skydives and 0 BASE jumps, far below the accepted minimum of 200+ skydives and proper BASE progression before attempting any BASE jump, making this a clear case of grossly insufficient experience. Attempting a slider-up cliff jump with a tracking suit with no BASE experience and minimal skydiving experience represents multiple severe human errors in progression, judgment, and preparation. The narrative indicates an unstable exit and/or excessive delay leading to insufficient separation and subterminal impact near the exit point.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attempted BASE jump with no experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted a slider-up cliff BASE jump with a tracking suit having only 5 skydives, zero BASE jumps, and zero wingsuit or tracking suit skydiving experience meeting accepted minimums. This decision to attempt a terminal cliff jump - requiring sub-terminal tracking, slider-up deployment skills, and object avoidance capability - with virtually no relevant training or experience was the direct producing mechanism of the fatal outcome. The accepted minimum is 200+ skydives before beginning BASE, with extensive tracking suit skydiving experience required before BASE use, yet the jumper had 5 total skydives.

L2_preconditions.label: No BASE or skydiving training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 5 skydives, zero BASE jumps, zero tracking suit skydiving experience, and no wingsuit experience. The accepted minimum is 200+ skydives before beginning BASE, with 40-50 tracking suit skydives required before BASE use of a tracking suit. This near-total absence of training and experience meant the jumper lacked every foundational skill required for the jump: freefall body control, tracking, canopy piloting, deployment technique, and object avoidance.

L0_description: The fatality resulted from a packing error: the jumper packed the pilot chute inside the main container instead of routing it to the BOC pouch, causing a total malfunction (PC trapped inside closed container). Additionally, the jumper chose to jump in deteriorating weather with increasing winds and rain, which constitutes a decision to jump in conditions that accepted practice would advise against.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Packing memory failure - PC stowed in container
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During packing, the jumper placed the pilot chute inside the main container on top of the folded canopy instead of routing it out to the BOC pouch. This was a memory/attention failure during a known procedural sequence — the jumper knew the correct procedure but failed to execute the critical step of routing the PC to the BOC. When the BOC handle was pulled on jump, no PC was attached, resulting in a total malfunction and fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to maintain sufficient attention during the packing sequence, resulting in the pilot chute being stowed inside the container rather than routed to the BOC pouch. This is a lapse in attention management during a procedural task — the jumper knew the correct procedure but failed to execute the critical step, indicating a breakdown in attentional monitoring during packing.

L2_preconditions.label: Deteriorating weather impaired packing focus
L2_preconditions.description: The weather was deteriorating with increasing wind and rain during the afternoon. While the narrative states the weather was a 'red herring' regarding the proximate cause of death, the stressful environmental conditions — packing in worsening weather with pressure to decide whether to jump or hike down — plausibly degraded the jumper's concentration during the packing process, contributing to the critical procedural omission.

L0_description: The report contains virtually no information: no accident narrative, no possible factors, no weather, no equipment details, no experience data, and no cause of death. There is insufficient information to support either a human error classification or an other cause classification.

The narrative provides minimal information: the jumper impacted talus below the exit with the canopy out, and there were no witnesses. No details are available regarding experience, equipment, packing, weather, body position, or the sequence of events leading to impact. There is insufficient information to deduce a human error pathway or to identify a specific non-human cause.

Two experienced witnesses provide contradictory accounts — one reports a late pull, the other reports a pilot chute in tow / bridle issue with an early pull. The gear was not available for inspection, making it impossible to determine whether the cause was a human error (late deployment) or an equipment malfunction. The narrative explicitly states there is no way to reconcile the two accounts, and insufficient information exists to support either 001HE or 002OC.

The jumper maintained an active body position throughout freefall but never attempted deployment, with no equipment malfunction, no medical cause identified by autopsy, and no environmental factors. The narrative explicitly states 'Nobody knows why he did not pull.' There is insufficient information to deduce a human error pathway or to identify a positive non-human cause.

The narrative is a memorial tribute and contains no technical details about the accident sequence, equipment malfunction, decision-making errors, or environmental conditions at the time of the jump. The cause of death is listed as impact in terminal freefall and 'proximity flying' is noted as a possible factor, but the narrative explicitly states they are seeking more information. There is insufficient information to classify this as either human error or other cause.

The jumper was found dead with canopy deployed, but nobody witnessed the jump, no gear inspection was conducted before authorities removed it, and weather conditions were not reported. All proposed explanations (off-heading, packing issue, thermals) are explicitly described as speculation. There is insufficient evidence to support either a specific human error pathway or a non-human cause.

The jumper went missing and was found months later with no witnesses, no known jump date, no weather information, and no evidence of what occurred. The narrative explicitly states it is impossible to determine what happened. While the cliff is known for unpredictable crosswinds, there is no evidence establishing whether wind was a factor, nor is there evidence establishing human error. Neither 001HE nor 002OC can be supported.

The narrative provides virtually no information about the jump, the jumper, the equipment, or the circumstances of the fatality. The account is based entirely on secondhand hiker reports, with no details on gear, packing, weather, experience, or sequence of events. There is insufficient information to support either a human error classification or an other cause classification.

The jumper clearly pulled too late, resulting in impact at line stretch. However, the narrative explicitly states there is no video and no way to determine whether the cause was sun glare impairing visual altitude judgment, unexpected suit performance characteristics, attentional fixation, or some other factor. Multiple competing hypotheses exist with no evidence to distinguish among them, and none can be supported with sufficient confidence to classify as 001HE or 002OC.

The narrative explicitly states the cause of death was 'Misrigging' and that 'it is not a manufacturer error,' indicating the malfunction resulted from the jumper's own rigging or packing actions rather than an equipment defect. This constitutes human error in equipment preparation that led to a fatal malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Misrigging equipment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The cause of death is listed as 'Malfunction, Misrigging' and the narrative explicitly states it was not a manufacturer error, indicating the jumper incorrectly rigged his own equipment. This misrigging produced a malfunction that he was unable to resolve in the 8 seconds before impact. The rigging error was the direct mechanism that created the fatal malfunction.
